questions.question: Define relational operators in Java and list all six operators with their meanings.
keyPoints: Relational operators are binary operators that compare two values and return boolean
List: == (equal to), != (not equal to), > (greater than), < (less than), >= (greater than or equal to), <= (less than or equal to)
All return boolean (true/false)

questions.question: Explain the difference between == operator and equals() method when comparing Strings in Java with an example.
keyPoints: == compares references (memory addresses), equals() compares content
For String literals, == may return true due to string pooling
Example: new String("Hello") == "Hello" returns false but .equals() returns true
Always use equals() for content comparison

questions.question: What is short-circuit evaluation in Java? Explain with an example using relational operators.
keyPoints: Short-circuit: if result can be determined from first operand, second is not evaluated
In &&: if first is false, second is skipped (result must be false)
In ||: if first is true, second is skipped (result must be true)
Example: if(obj != null && obj.value > 5) - avoids NullPointerException

questions.question: Can relational operators >, <, >=, <= be used with boolean values in Java? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: No, these four operators cannot be used with boolean values
Only == and != can be used with booleans
Reason: booleans don't have natural ordering
Using > or < with boolean causes compile-time error

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate all six relational operators with integer operands and print their results.
keyPoints: Use variables like a=10, b=20
Demonstrate: a==b, a!=b, a>b, a<b, a>=b, a<=b
Output should show boolean results for each operation
Show correct output format

questions.question: Explain with an example why comparing floating-point numbers using == operator may give unexpected results.
keyPoints: Floating-point numbers have precision issues due to binary representation
Should use tolerance/range for comparison: Math.abs(a-b) < 0.0001
Not unique to Java, applies to all programming languages

questions.question: What is string pooling in Java and how does it affect == comparison of String literals?
keyPoints: String pooling: Java stores string literals in a common pool to save memory
When creating String with literal, JVM checks pool first
If same literal exists, returns same reference
Therefore "Hello" == "Hello" is true (same reference)

questions.question: Explain all six relational operators in Java with examples. Include a table showing results when comparing a=15 and b=25. Also explain why relational operators are essential in conditional programming.
keyPoints: Define each operator with syntax and return type
Create comparison table with a=15, b=25 showing all true/false results
Explain: == (false), != (true), > (false), < (true), >= (false), <= (true)
Discuss use in if-else, while loops, for conditions
Mention they form backbone of decision-making

questions.question: Write a detailed note on == operator versus equals() method in Java. Include: 1) How == works with primitives and objects 2) How equals() works by default and when overridden 3) Common pitfalls with String comparison 4) Best practices for comparisons.
keyPoints: == with primitives: compares values; == with objects: compares references
equals() default: inherited from Object, same as ==; String.equals(): overridden to compare content
Pitfall: new String() creates new object, == returns false even with same content
Best practices: use equals() for strings, == for enum check, Objects.equals() for null-safety

questions.question: Write a Java program that takes an integer score as input and uses relational operators to determine the grade: score>=90(A), >=80(B), >=70(C), >=60(D), else(F). Also explain how relational operators work with logical operators in this program.
keyPoints: Create Scanner for input
Use if-else if chain with relational operators (>=)
Explain: each condition checked sequentially
Use logical &&: e.g., score>=80 && score<90 for clarity
Demonstrate short-circuit evaluation in conditions
Show sample output for different scores

questions.question: Discuss common mistakes and pitfalls when using relational operators in Java. Include: 1) Using == for String comparison 2) Comparing floating-point numbers 3) Chaining relational operators 4) Comparing incompatible types. Provide solutions for each.
keyPoints: Pitfall 1: String == compares references; Solution: use equals()
Pitfall 2: Floating-point precision; Solution: use tolerance comparison
Pitfall 3: Cannot chain (a<b<c); Solution: use logical operators (a<b && b<c)
Pitfall 4: Comparing incompatible types; Solution: ensure type compatibility
Additional: null comparisons, auto-boxing issues with Integer
